description:
Context: Default keymaps are currently <leader>jfr<...> (e.g. start/stop/dump/check). Re-evaluate for shorter mappings while avoiding common clashes.

Current default (<leader>jfr...)
Pros:
- Namespaced; low collision risk
- Self-documenting (jfr)
Cons:
- Long to type; repetitive
- Competes with user-specific leader maps; slower for frequent use

Constraints / clash-avoidance
- Avoid very common prefixes: <leader>f (find), <leader>g (git), <leader>b (buffer), <leader>t (test/terminal), <leader>c (code), <leader>l (lsp)
- Avoid overriding built-in or popular plugin defaults (telescope, gitsigns, neotest, harpoon, oil)
- Keep groupable for which-key (single prefix)

Alternative mapping schemes to consider
1) <leader>jr<...> (shorter namespace)
   - start: <leader>jrs
   - stop:  <leader>jrx
   - dump:  <leader>jrd
   - check: <leader>jrc
   - status:<leader>jrt (or <leader>jrr)
   Pros: close to current; minimal migration; still low clash.

2) <leader>jf<...> (jfr with 2 keys)
   - start: <leader>jfs
   - stop:  <leader>jfx
   - dump:  <leader>jfd
   - check: <leader>jfc
   - status:<leader>jft
   Pros: mnemonic (JFR); shorter than jfr.
   Cons: <leader>jf might be used by users for 'jump/find'.

3) <leader>j<...> submap (very short, higher clash risk)
   - start: <leader>js
   - stop:  <leader>jx
   - dump:  <leader>jd
   - check: <leader>jc
   - status:<leader>jt
   Pros: fastest.
   Cons: <leader>j is often used for custom navigation; higher collision.

4) Opt-in preset: no defaults unless config.keymaps.enabled=true
   - Provide suggested defaults in README and which-key integration only when enabled.
   Pros: eliminates clashes by default.
   Cons: less out-of-box ergonomics.

Decision criteria
- Ergonomics for frequent workflows (start/stop/dump/check/status)
- Clash likelihood with common Neovim ecosystems
- Migration cost (docs, config defaults)
- Which-key friendliness

Next steps
- Pick one scheme as default (or switch to opt-in) and update defaults + README accordingly.
notes: Deferred: keymap shortening is secondary; revisit after core command UX is settled.
title: Decision: shorten default key bindings (currently <leader>jfr...)

description:
User reports :JFRStart/:JFRStatus picker shows 4 JVMs (jdtls, spring app, quarkus, maven wrapper) even though only 2 should be considered project-level (maven wrapper + spring boot app).

Likely causes:
- Project root detection returning nil, causing commands.lua to fall back to global jvm.list() (which excludes the jcmd process itself, hence 4/5).
- Or system_properties probe matching too broadly (e.g. user.dir equals root for tooling like jdtls), requiring exclusion rules.

Next steps:
- Add a debug message or health hint when project_scoped_jvms enabled but project.get_root() is nil (showing global JVM list).
- Tighten project scoping match: prefer only maven.multiModuleProjectDirectory + user.dir; make sun.java.command opt-in.
- Add default exclude patterns for obvious tooling (jdtls/equinox) from project-scoped list.

Repro data (jcmd -l): 29169 equinox/jdtls, 28531 Application, 23334 Quarkus GeneratedMain, 27822 MavenWrapperMain, plus jcmd itself.
title: Project-scoped JVM list includes non-project JVMs / falls back to global

description:
Context: Current name is 'nvim-jfr' (Java Flight Recorder integration for Neovim). Re-evaluate naming for discoverability, clarity, and ecosystem conventions.

Current name (nvim-jfr)
Pros:
- Clear to Java/JFR users; matches common acronym
- Indicates Neovim target (nvim- prefix)
- Short, easy to type
Cons:
- Acronym opaque to non-Java users (search/discovery)
- Hyphenated prefix naming is less common with lazy.nvim specs (usually 'author/repo') but fine
- Could be confused with 'JFR' meaning other things
- Harder to guess capabilities (JMC integration, recordings UI) from name

Alternative names to consider
- jfr.nvim (short, conventional; loses explicit nvim prefix)
- flight-recorder.nvim / nvim-flight-recorder (more descriptive; longer)
- nvim-flightrecorder (no hyphen; still descriptive)
- jfr-tools.nvim (signals a toolkit; broader than just start/stop)
- mission-control.nvim / nvim-jmc (if JMC becomes primary; may mislead if still mostly JFR)
- jfrctl.nvim (CLI/control vibe; niche)

Decision criteria
- Searchability on GitHub + within Neovim plugin lists
- Clarity for non-JFR users
- Namespace stability (Lua module path, docs, commands) and migration cost

Next steps
- Pick top 2-3 candidates, sanity-check for name collisions, and decide whether to keep repo as-is or rename with compatibility shim.
notes: Deferred: naming/renaming is disruptive; postpone until core workflow is stable.
title: Decision: rethink project/plugin name

description:
Set up GitHub-side project management + CI pipelines so nvim-jfr has consistent checks, contribution scaffolding, and visible status.

Deliverables
- GitHub Actions workflows:
  - CI (push + PR): run headless tests on a matrix of OS + Neovim versions
  - Optional lint/format job(s): stylua/luacheck (or equivalent) if adopted
  - Optional release workflow (tag -> GitHub Release) deferred unless explicitly desired
- Repo hygiene for collaboration:
  - CONTRIBUTING.md (how to run the same checks locally)
  - Issue templates (bug/feature), PR template
  - README: CI badge + short dev section
- GitHub Project (v2): Backlog/In Progress/Done views + basic fields (Status/Priority/Type/Area) and automation (issue closed/PR merged -> Done).

Open questions / decisions
- What test runner is canonical for this repo (plenary/busted/minitest/custom)? CI should call a single entrypoint (e.g. scripts/test or make test).
- Supported Neovim versions: README says 0.8+; decide CI matrix (e.g. stable + nightly, and/or pinned 0.9/0.10/0.11).
- Include windows-latest now (README claims cross-platform) vs add later.

Suggested subtasks
1) Add a single test entrypoint command used locally + in CI
2) Add CI workflow with OS+Neovim matrix
3) Add lint/format checks + config files (if adopted)
4) Add CONTRIBUTING + templates
5) Create GitHub Project + automation + link epics
notes: Deferred: useful, but not on the critical path for core recording UX + reducing command overload.
title: Feature: GitHub project + CI pipelines for nvim-jfr

close_reason: Resolved earlier: project JVM probe tuned (timeouts/max_probes/time_budget + caching + heuristics). Reopened accidentally; closing.
description:
When project_scoped_jvms raw matching yields none, we probe `jcmd <pid> VM.system_properties` to recover Maven/Spring Boot JVMs. This can delay opening the picker by ~3–4s when multiple JVMs are running.

Goal: keep detection but make the picker open fast.

Approach ideas:
- Short timeouts and fewer probes (defaults), stop early once a match is found.
- Cache negative/timeout results so subsequent invocations are instant.
- Probe likely candidates first (Maven/Gradle wrapper, etc.).
- (Optional later) async probing + picker refresh.

Acceptance: picker opens in <500ms in common cases; detection still works.
title: Reduce lag when probing system_properties for project JVMs

Project-scoped JVM filtering is best-effort; Maven wrapper may omit project path (tracked separately).
